notes: searches: ~10 (10 shared, 0 stragglers). scholarship/ contains no texts on biblical composition dating (INDEX.md checked; the 19th-century-source shelf covers Reformation/Sabbath, not Pentateuch criticism). Chain-inheritance note for synthesis: GC-00-007, GC-00-008 and GC-01-097 share ONE evidential anchor (the date of pentateuchal composition), while GC-00-005 fails on a DISTINCT anchor (antiquity of human history) — 4 accusatory rulings, 2 independent evidential failure points. period_standard candidates for synthesis tagging: GC-00-005, GC-00-008, GC-01-097 (Ussher-margin chronology and Mosaic dating were standard in White's milieu and in her named sources; academic criticism had moved by 1888 — de Wette 1805, Wellhausen 1878 — but her confessional sources had not). GC-17-066 is arguably period_standard too (19th-c. scholarship commonly dated Joel 9th–8th c.), but it needs no excuse since it is ruled genuinely-open. Defense objects exist only for GC-01-097 (answered in its record per §7.4); the defender skipped GC-00-005/007/008 and GC-17-066 as already-cautious disputed verdicts.
evidence_review: The dossier's shared question is the composition history of the biblical books, on which the state of the question has moved decisively since White's sources were written, along two separable evidential lines. LINE 1 — antiquity of human history (bears on GC-00-005 alone). The 'first 2,500 years of human history' frame presupposes the Ussher-type computation (creation 4004 BC, Moses/Exodus c. 1491 BC) printed in KJV margins from 1701. James Barr's Ethel M. Wood Lecture 'Biblical Chronology: Legend or Science?' (Univ. of London, 1987) establishes that the biblical chronology is a literary-theological construct, not a historical measurement. Independently, the archaeological record documents continuous human settlement long before 4004 BC: Pre-Pottery Neolithic Jericho by c. 9000 BC, Çatalhöyük c. 7400–6200 BC (UNESCO; radiocarbon plus dendrochronological calibration), literate urban civilization at Uruk by c. 3350–3200 BC (Met Museum, 'Origins of Writing'; Archaeology Magazine 2016), and Homo sapiens some 300,000 years old. No peer-reviewed archaeology defends a c. 4000 BC start of human history; the sole defense is young-earth creationism (Answers in Genesis, ICR), a confessional position whose case rests on scriptural premises plus rejection of the convergent chronometers, without standing in any independent venue. Note the asymmetry: the claim's OTHER component — no written Scripture existed before Moses' era — is true on every scholarly dating, since critical scholarship moves biblical composition LATER, not earlier. LINE 2 — date of biblical composition (bears on GC-00-007, GC-00-008, GC-01-097, and, separately, GC-17-066). The academic preponderance places the earliest biblical literary composition in the first millennium BC: epigraphically, an Old Hebrew script and standardized scribal infrastructure capable of long literary prose is attested only from the 9th century onward (Rollston, Writing and Literacy in the World of Ancient Israel, SBL 2010), and Schniedewind (How the Bible Became a Book, Cambridge UP 2004) argues biblical literature was written down largely in the 8th–6th centuries; literary-historically, the completed Torah is dated to the Persian period (Römer, 'How to Date Pentateuchal Texts', FAT 111, Mohr Siebeck 2016; the compositional process, though 'hotly contested' in its details, is contested within a 1st-millennium window). For Deuteronomy specifically, the 7th-century Josianic connection (de Wette 1805 onward) is reinforced by the close parallels between Deut 28:20–44 and Esarhaddon's Succession Treaty of 672 BC (Weinfeld, Biblica 46, 1965; Steymans, OBO 1995), strengthened by the 2009 Tell Tayinat find of an EST copy imposed on western vassals (Steymans, 'Deuteronomy 28 and Tell Tayinat', 2013) — the very chapter GC quotes is the most firmly 7th-century-anchored text in the Pentateuch. The named minority — Kitchen (On the Reliability of the Old Testament, Eerdmans 2003: 2nd-millennium Hittite treaty form, Egyptian background), Berman (JBL: Hittite CTH 133 closer to Deut 13 than EST), Zehnder's critique of direct EST dependence — is methodologically argued and was engaged: Levinson & Stackert's response shows the form-parallel argument cannot date texts by itself; the linguistic profile of pentateuchal prose is monarchic-period Classical Hebrew, not 2nd-millennium; and even scholars who doubt direct EST literary dependence (Crouch; Zehnder's sympathizers) retain a 1st-millennium date. Defense of actual Mosaic-era composition today is confined almost entirely to confessional venues (JETS, Themelios), so the disagreement fails the charter's genuinely-open test (§1: disagreement must not reduce to confessional alignment). Consequences for the arithmetic: the '1600 years Moses to John' span requires the Mosaic anchor (c. 1500 BC to c. AD 100); on critical dating the span of composition-as-books is roughly 900–1100 years (archaic poetry possibly 12th–10th c. — Cross/Freedman, Russell 2007, itself debated with proposals down to the 5th c. — to Revelation under Domitian c. 95 AD, which is mainstream on all sides). JOEL is the exception in the pool: its date is notoriously underdetermined — proposals run from the 9th to the 2nd century BC on purely internal evidence; the current majority favors the Persian period (Wolff, Hermeneia: 500–350; Crenshaw, Anchor Bible: 5th c.; Barton, OTL: c. 400), but pre-exilic dates are defended on comparable internal evidence by non-confessional critics (Kapelrud c. 600; Rudolph 597–587) and by evangelicals (Stuart, WBC: 9th–8th c.; the nations-mentioned argument and the absence of Persian loanwords cut early, the temple-community profile and prophetic citations cut late; Thompson's Festschrift survey 'The Date of Joel' documents the standoff). A '2,500 years before 1780' figure implies c. 720 BC (with rounding, c. 620–820 BC), inside the published range though not the majority position. WHAT CHANGED SINCE THE 19TH CENTURY: in White's milieu the Ussher margins and Mosaic authorship were standard, and 19th-century critics themselves commonly dated Joel early (9th–8th c.); the Deuteronomy 7th-century dating existed academically (de Wette) but had not penetrated her confessional sources. The 1600-year span was and remains the standard figure of conservative Bible handbooks.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет рамку композиции Библии в «Великой борьбе»: письменное откровение началось с Моисея, Библию писали 1600 лет от Моисея до Иоанна, Иоиль писал за 2500 лет до 1780 года, а предупреждение Второзакония прозвучало за четырнадцать веков до разрушения Иерусалима в 70 году нашей эры. Современная библеистика датирует древнейшие библейские книги первым тысячелетием до нашей эры, а ядро Второзакония связывает с седьмым веком, поэтому четыре утверждения из пяти в написанном виде получили оценки от «маловероятно» до «опровергнуто»; опровергнута только рамка «первые 2500 лет человеческой истории», требующая начала человечества около 4000 года до нашей эры. Датировка Иоиля остаётся честно открытым вопросом: специалисты предлагают даты от девятого до четвёртого века до нашей эры, и цифра «2500 лет» попадает внутрь этого диапазона. В ослабленных версиях (Библия складывалась около тысячелетия и дольше, предупреждение Второзакония на много веков старше 70 года) четыре утверждения из пяти хорошо подтверждены. Картину изменили бы датируемые рукописи или надписи второго тысячелетия до нашей эры с библейским текстом.

quantifier_audit: the first twenty-five hundred years of human history

components.component: Human history's first 2,500 years ran from its beginning to the era of Moses — i.e., humanity began c. 4000 BC (the Ussher-type arithmetic frame asserted as world history)
components.rationale: Decisively refuted by convergent, independent evidence: continuous archaeological record of human settlement from Pre-Pottery Neolithic Jericho (c. 9000 BC) and Çatalhöyük (c. 7400–6200 BC) through literate Uruk (c. 3350–3200 BC), calibrated by radiocarbon and dendrochronology; Homo sapiens c. 300,000 years old. Barr (1987) shows the 2,500-year Adam-to-Moses figure is a correct rendering of the Bible's internal genealogical arithmetic but not a measurement of human history. No peer-reviewed archaeology supports a c. 4000 BC start.

components.component: No biblical text existed in written form before the era traditionally assigned to Moses (c. 15th–13th c. BC); transmission of religious tradition before then was oral
components.rationale: True on every scholarly dating and strengthened by the critical position: conservative dating begins biblical writing with Moses; critical dating begins it centuries later (9th–6th c. BC). Either way, nothing of the Bible existed in writing before c. 1500 BC, and oral transmission preceding written Scripture is a commonplace across the field.

components.component: What God communicated in that period counts as 'revelation' (divine communication through chosen servants)
components.rationale: Theological category; out of scope per §9. The empirical rulings above are conditional on reading 'written revelation' as 'written Scripture', which the paragraph's own context (Moses to John, an inspired book) requires.

rulings.weakened_version: No biblical book yet existed during the long pre-Mosaic era — a span the Bible's own traditional chronology reckons at about 2,500 years — and religious knowledge in that era passed down orally.
rulings.minority_engagement: The only standing defense of a c. 4000 BC start of human history is young-earth creationism (Answers in Genesis, ICR), which argues that Ussher's method was scholarly by his era's standards and that uniformitarian dating methods rest on unprovable assumptions. The first point is true but irrelevant (period-competence does not make the chronology correct); the second fails on evidence, because the chronometers are multiple and independent (dendrochronology, ice cores, varves, calibrated radiocarbon, Egyptian/Mesopotamian king-lists) and converge on the same deep timescale, while YEC offers no positive counter-chronology accepted in any peer-reviewed venue. Confessional identity is not the ground of dismissal; the absence of independent evidential support is. No Phase-1 defense object exists (defender skipped disputed verdicts); the strongest defense — that White writes devotionally within her tradition's chronology — is incorporated as the weakened version, ruled well-supported.

sources.ref: James Barr, 'Biblical Chronology: Legend or Science?' (Ethel M. Wood Lecture, University of London, 1987), biblicalstudies.org.uk/pdf/emwl/chronology_barr.pdf
sources.independence_note: Hebrew-Bible specialist analyzing the Ussher-type computation from the biblical side; genealogically independent of the archaeological line.

sources.ref: UNESCO World Heritage entry 'Neolithic Site of Çatalhöyük' (occupation 7400–6200 BC) + PMC article on Pre-Pottery Neolithic Jericho (c. 9000 BC)
sources.independence_note: Archaeological/scientific line resting on excavation and radiometric data; independent of biblical-chronology scholarship.

sources.ref: Metropolitan Museum of Art, 'The Origins of Writing'; Archaeology Magazine, 'The World's Oldest Writing' (2016): proto-cuneiform at Uruk c. 3350–3200 BC
sources.independence_note: Digest of the Uruk epigraphic corpus; same genealogy as other Uruk summaries, counted once; used for the continuous-civilization context, not as the decisive counterexample.

sources.ref: NCSE, 'Seven Myths about Ussher'; Wikipedia 'Ussher chronology' / 'Young Earth creationism'
sources.independence_note: Background on the chronology's reception; genealogically dependent on the mainstream literature above.

sources.ref: Answers in Genesis, 'The World: Born in 4004 BC?'; ICR, 'James Ussher and His Chronology'
sources.independence_note: The minority defense engaged; confessional apologetic, cited as position only.

rulings.would_change_this: Peer-reviewed evidence collapsing the convergent chronometric methods together — e.g., a demonstrated systematic error inflating dendrochronological and ice-core chronologies by thousands of years — or archaeological redating placing Neolithic Jericho and Çatalhöyük after c. 4000 BC. For the weakened version: a datable pre-1500 BC manuscript of any biblical text would refute it in the other direction.
rulings.notes: Genre: devotional Introduction, not a historical chapter. period_standard candidate: the Ussher frame stood in KJV margins and throughout White's milieu; academic prehistory had already moved by 1888/1911, but her confessional sources had not. The framing_gap of 4 is the dossier's largest and is the real finding here.

components.component: The earliest biblical books were composed in written form in the time of Moses (c. 15th–13th c. BC)
components.rationale: Clear academic preponderance against: Old Hebrew script and a standardized scribal apparatus capable of long literary prose are attested only from the 9th c. BC (Rollston); biblical literature was written down largely in the 8th–6th c. (Schniedewind); the completed Torah dates to the Persian period (Römer). Even the neo-documentarians who date sources earliest place them in the monarchy, not the Late Bronze Age. The linguistic profile of pentateuchal prose is monarchic Classical Hebrew. The engaged minority (Kitchen) fails to establish 2nd-millennium composition as opposed to 2nd-millennium memory.

components.component: 'The written word' — the theological status of these texts as inspired revelation
components.rationale: Out of scope per §9; the empirical layer ruled above is when the biblical texts were composed, conditional on reading 'the written word' as 'the biblical text', which the extraction and context support.

rulings.weakened_version: Some material now in the Pentateuch — archaic poetry and early legal and narrative tradition — may descend from traditions reaching back toward the late second millennium BC, though the biblical books as written compositions date to the monarchic period and later.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest opposing position: K. A. Kitchen (On the Reliability of the Old Testament, Eerdmans 2003), an academically credentialed Egyptologist arguing that the Sinai/Deuteronomy covenant form matches late-2nd-millennium Hittite treaties (historical prologue plus blessings and curses) rather than 1st-millennium Assyrian ones, and that Egyptian customs and nomenclature in the Pentateuch fit 2nd-millennium realities. The argument is real but fails to carry composition-dating: (1) treaty-form parallels cannot date texts by themselves (Levinson & Stackert contra Berman), and the closest verbal parallels for Deut 28 run to the 7th-c. Esarhaddon treaty, not Hittite exemplars; (2) accurate 2nd-millennium background at most shows old tradition or memory, not Mosaic-era writing; (3) the epigraphic record shows no Hebrew literary infrastructure before the 9th c. (Rollston). Because actual Mosaic-era composition is today defended almost entirely within confessional venues, the disagreement reduces largely to confessional alignment and fails the genuinely-open test; hence improbable, not genuinely-open. No Phase-1 defense object exists (defender skipped disputed verdicts).

sources.ref: W. M. Schniedewind, How the Bible Became a Book (Cambridge UP, 2004): biblical literature written down largely 8th–6th c. BC
sources.independence_note: Social history of writing; engaged via published summaries.

sources.ref: C. A. Rollston, Writing and Literacy in the World of Ancient Israel (SBL, 2010) with published reviews (ProQuest; BAS)
sources.independence_note: Iron Age epigraphic corpus; same broad data class as Schniedewind but independent author and analysis (dates Old Hebrew script later than the consensus, strengthening the point).

sources.ref: T. Römer, 'How to Date Pentateuchal Texts — Some Case Studies', in The Formation of the Pentateuch (FAT 111, Mohr Siebeck, 2016)
sources.independence_note: European literary-historical criticism; a line genealogically distinct from the epigraphic one.

sources.ref: K. A. Kitchen, On the Reliability of the Old Testament (Eerdmans, 2003) with CBQ 69 (2007) review
sources.independence_note: The engaged minority; academically credentialed, confessionally located defense.

sources.ref: F. M. Cross & D. N. Freedman on archaic poetry; B. D. Russell, The Song of the Sea (2007): 12th–10th c. datings vs archaizing-style skeptics (range 13th–5th c.)
sources.independence_note: Grounds the weakened ruling; a debate internal to non-confessional scholarship, hence genuinely open.

sources.ref: 'Composition of the Torah' — Wikipedia
sources.independence_note: Digest of the literature above; not counted toward the 2-source floor.

rulings.would_change_this: A datable 2nd-millennium Hebrew (or proto-Hebrew) manuscript or inscription containing pentateuchal text, or a peer-reviewed linguistic demonstration that pentateuchal prose reflects 2nd-millennium Northwest Semitic rather than monarchic Classical Hebrew. For the weakened version: a consensus resolution of the archaic-poetry dating debate in either direction.
rulings.notes: The smallest framing gap in the dossier (1): even the steelman only reaches genuinely-open, because the claim's subject is the beginning of biblical WRITING, and no relaxation short of changing the subject makes Mosaic-era writing probable.

components.component: Moses was 'the historian of creation and the law' — i.e., Moses wrote Genesis and the pentateuchal law
components.rationale: Same evidential anchor as GC-00-007 (see evidence_review, Line 2): composition of Genesis and the law is dated to the monarchic-through-Persian periods by the clear preponderance of academic scholarship; Mosaic authorship is defended almost entirely in confessional venues.

components.component: Biblical composition concluded with the Johannine writings near the end of the 1st century AD
components.rationale: Revelation is mainstream-dated to Domitian's reign (c. 95 AD) and the Johannine corpus to c. 90–110; the endpoint holds on all sides. (Whether the author was John the apostle is a separate, contested attribution question, not load-bearing for the endpoint date; a minority extends 2 Peter into the early 2nd c., which would stretch, not shrink, the span.)

components.component: The total span of biblical composition was about 1,600 years
components.rationale: Pure arithmetic on the two anchors: c. 1500 BC to c. 100 AD gives 1,600 only if the Mosaic anchor holds. On critical dating the span of composition-as-books is roughly 900–1,100 years (10th/9th c. BC at the earliest, counting archaic poetry, to c. 100 AD). The figure inherits the improbability of the Mosaic anchor.

rulings.weakened_version: The Bible was composed over an immense span — roughly a millennium or more — by many writers across changing eras, concluding near the end of the first century AD.
rulings.minority_engagement: The 1,600-year figure is the standard span of conservative Bible handbooks (commonly '1,500–1,600 years, 40+ authors') and holds exactly if Kitchen's 2nd-millennium case for Mosaic composition holds; that case is engaged and found wanting at GC-00-007 (treaty-form parallels cannot date texts; epigraphic and linguistic evidence points to 1st-millennium composition). The claim is therefore a conditional frame whose condition fails on the preponderance of evidence, while the same rhetorical point ('one coherent book written across many centuries by many hands') survives fully at the millennium scale — which is why the weakened ruling is well-supported and uncontested. No Phase-1 defense object exists (defender skipped disputed verdicts).

sources.ref: Shared pool, evidence_review Line 2: Schniedewind (Cambridge UP 2004), Rollston (SBL 2010), Römer (Mohr Siebeck 2016) for the start anchor
sources.independence_note: Same anchor as GC-00-007; chain-inherited, counted as one failure point in synthesis.

sources.ref: 'Dating the Bible' — Wikipedia (earliest texts to Daniel 168–164 BC; NT 2nd half of 1st c.; Revelation under Domitian)
sources.independence_note: Digest of standard datings; used for the endpoint and span arithmetic, where it matches the academic literature.

sources.ref: Biblical Archaeology Society, 'When Was the Bible Written?'
sources.independence_note: Popular-scholarly digest, genealogically close to the academic mainstream; corroborates the millennium-scale span.

sources.ref: K. A. Kitchen, On the Reliability of the Old Testament (Eerdmans, 2003)
sources.independence_note: The minority under which the 1,600-year figure holds; engaged at GC-00-007.

rulings.would_change_this: The same evidence class as GC-00-007: a datable 2nd-millennium witness to pentateuchal text would restore the 1,600-year span; conversely, a consensus shift dating ALL biblical material after the exile (a live minimalist position) would shrink the span below a millennium and lower the weakened ruling.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate: the 1,600-year figure was the standard span in 19th-century conservative handbooks and remains so in confessional literature today. Favorable component ruling recorded (endpoint well-supported) per §7 symmetry.

components.component: The book of Joel was written about 2,500 years before 1780 — i.e., c. 720 BC (with rounding, c. 620–820 BC)
components.rationale: Joel's date is among the most disputed in the prophetic corpus, argued entirely from internal evidence: proposals run from the 9th to the 2nd c. BC. The current majority favors the Persian period (Wolff: 500–350; Crenshaw: 5th c.; Barton: c. 400), but pre-exilic dates are defended on comparable internal evidence by non-confessional critics (Kapelrud c. 600; Rudolph 597–587) and evangelicals (Stuart: 9th–8th c.); the disagreement does not reduce to confessional alignment, and specialists on both sides concede the evidence underdetermines the answer (no king mentioned, no Assyria/Babylon/Persia among the nations, no Persian loanwords — cutting early; functioning temple community, citations of earlier prophets, Greeks in Joel 3:6 — cutting late). The implied c. 720 BC sits inside the published range, in the minority pre-exilic window.

rulings.weakened_version: Joel was written well over two thousand years before 1780.
rulings.minority_engagement: Not strictly required for genuinely-open, but both sides are documented per §1: the post-exilic majority (Wolff, Crenshaw, Barton) rests on the book's temple-community profile and its citations of other prophets; the pre-exilic case (Thompson's survey; Kapelrud; Rudolph; Stuart) rests on the roster of nations and the linguistic absence of Persian traces. Neither side commands decisive evidence; head-counting favors post-exilic, but the charter forbids counting heads where the evidence base is thin and shared (§5.4). For the weakened version's marginal contestation: Treves's 2nd-c.-BC (Maccabean) dating would push the span to c. 1,940 years, but it is a fringe position without following; on all mainstream datings (9th c.–350 BC) the span is 2,130–2,615 years. No Phase-1 defense object exists (defender skipped disputed verdicts); the verifier's period note (19th-c. scholarship commonly dated Joel 9th–8th c.) is incorporated below.

sources.ref: J. A. Thompson, 'The Date of Joel' (Festschrift article; biblicalstudies.org.uk/pdf/myers/thompson.pdf) — surveys Kapelrud (c. 600), Rudolph (597–587), and the early datings
sources.independence_note: Survey line documenting the pre-exilic side from non-confessional scholarship.

sources.ref: H. W. Wolff, Joel and Amos (Hermeneia): composite work 500–350 BC; J. L. Crenshaw, Joel (Anchor Bible, 1995): 5th c., dating 'guesswork'
sources.independence_note: The post-exilic majority line; independent commentary traditions (German form-critical vs American), though both argue from the same internal data — the shared data pool is the reason the question stays open.

sources.ref: 'The Date of Joel' — exegesisandtheology.com (2023): the 9th-c. case (nations mentioned/not mentioned; Obadiah–Joel–Amos citation order)
sources.independence_note: Conservative statement of the early-date arguments; cited as position, the same arguments appear in Stuart's WBC commentary.

sources.ref: 'Book of Joel' — Wikipedia; academia.edu, 'The Date and Meaning of the Book of Joel'
sources.independence_note: Range documentation (9th–2nd c. proposals); digest of the literature above.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed resolution of the intertextual direction of dependence (does Amos quote Joel or Joel quote Amos?), a firm linguistic-typological placement of Joel's Hebrew, or an external anchor (e.g., an identifiable historical referent for the locust crisis) would collapse the range; a firm Persian-period result would move the as-written ruling to improbable, a firm 8th-c. result to well-supported.
rulings.notes: The dossier's one genuinely-open ruling, and genuinely so: openness is documented on both sides, not a budget artifact. period_standard: the '2,500 years' figure matched the 19th-century scholarly mainstream (9th–8th-c. datings were then common), so even a future improbable ruling would carry the period_standard tag.

components.component: The Deuteronomy 28:56–57 warning was given/recorded about fourteen centuries before AD 70 (c. 1330 BC, Mosaic era)
components.rationale: The 7th-century dating of Deuteronomy's core (Deut 12–26 + 28) is among the most robust results of Pentateuchal criticism: the Josianic connection (de Wette onward), the close parallels between Deut 28:20–44 and Esarhaddon's Succession Treaty of 672 BC (Weinfeld 1965; Steymans 1995), and the 2009 Tell Tayinat find showing the EST imposed on western vassals. On the preponderance, the warning was written roughly seven, not fourteen, centuries before AD 70 — and the very chapter GC quotes is the most firmly 7th-century-anchored text in the Pentateuch. The engaged minority (Kitchen, Berman, Zehnder) does not overturn this: see minority_engagement.

components.component: 'Again was fulfilled the warning prophecy' — the mother-cannibalism of the AD 70 siege as fulfillment of Deut 28
components.rationale: Prophecy-fulfillment framing; out of scope per §9. (The Josephus siege events themselves were verified separately in Phase 1 and are not part of this claim.)

rulings.weakened_version: The Deuteronomy 28 warning was recorded many centuries — on any scholarly dating at least about seven — before the AD 70 siege it is said to foreshadow.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest opposing position: the 2nd-millennium dating defended by Kitchen (Hittite treaty form) and Berman (JBL: the 15th-c. Hittite text CTH 133 parallels Deut 13 more closely than the EST), with Zehnder's peer-published critique of direct EST literary dependence. Actual arguments engaged: Berman's parallel concerns Deut 13, not the ch. 28 curse sequence, where the EST parallels (sequence of curses, Deut 28:20–44) remain the closest known; Levinson & Stackert's response ('The Limitations of Resonance') shows form-resonance cannot carry composition-dating against the convergent linguistic and historical evidence; and even scholars who accept parts of the dependence critique (Crouch, Israel and the Assyrians) retain a 7th-century setting. The recent JETS survey (François, 'Is It Time to Abandon the Hittite Treaty Structure?') documents that the 2nd-millennium position survives today almost entirely within confessional venues. PHASE-1 DEFENDER ANSWERED (§7.4), point by point: (a) the Psalms-consistency complaint criticizes Phase-1 uniformity, not the evidence on Deuteronomy; adjudication rules on this claim's own evidence base (§0.2) — if anything it suggests the Psalm datings were under-scrutinized, not that this one was over-scrutinized; (b) 'White dates the utterance per internal chronology' — as printed, 'the warning prophecy given fourteen centuries before' asserts as history WHEN the warning was given, and asserting the narrative's internal chronology as the actual date presupposes exactly the Mosaic-era origin that the preponderance rejects; (c) 'composition remains hotly contested' — contested among academics is the compositional process within a 1st-millennium window, not a 15th–14th-c. composition; (d) 'the rhetorical function survives any dating' — conceded, and incorporated as the weakened version, ruled well-supported. The downgrade from anachronistic to disputed is thus partially vindicated: the claim is not simply false-by-consensus-with-no-debate, but the debate it points to does not extend to the 14-century figure.

sources.ref: M. Weinfeld, 'Traces of Assyrian Treaty Formulae in Deuteronomy', Biblica 46/4 (1965): 417–427
sources.independence_note: Origin of the EST-parallel line.

sources.ref: H. U. Steymans, Deuteronomium 28 und die adê zur Thronfolgeregelung Asarhaddons (OBO, 1995); Steymans, 'Deuteronomy 28 and Tell Tayinat' (2013)
sources.independence_note: Same comparative-Assyriological genealogy as Weinfeld, but strengthened by new PRIMARY evidence (Tayinat EST copy, 2009) — counted with Weinfeld as one line plus a primary anchor.

sources.ref: B. M. Levinson & J. Stackert, 'The Limitations of Resonance: A Response to Joshua Berman' (SSRN; J. Ancient Judaism)
sources.independence_note: Methodological line, independent of the Steymans genealogy; also documents the minority's actual argument.

sources.ref: J. Berman, JBL papers via 'Deuteronomy and Hittite Treaties' (bibleinterp.arizona.edu, 2014)
sources.independence_note: The peer-published minority itself; confessionally located (Bar-Ilan/orthodox) but venue-validated.

sources.ref: B. François, 'Is It Time to Abandon the Hittite Treaty Structure of Deuteronomy?', JETS 68.4
sources.independence_note: Confessional-venue survey conceding the state of the debate from the minority side; genealogically independent of the critical line.

sources.ref: De Wette's Josianic connection (2 Kings 22) as carried in standard reference literature ('Book of Deuteronomy' overviews)
sources.independence_note: The older, independent anchor of the 7th-c. date, predating and independent of the treaty-parallel line.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed demonstration that the Deut 28 curse sequence predates the Neo-Assyrian treaty tradition (e.g., via a datable earlier Northwest Semitic exemplar beyond Sefire), or any 2nd-millennium textual witness to Deuteronomy. Conversely, a consensus collapse of the Josianic connection toward a still-later (exilic) date would deepen the as-written ruling.
rulings.notes: Originally anachronistic, defense-downgraded to disputed. period_standard candidate: Mosaic dating was the standard of White's milieu and named sources, although de Wette's 7th-c. dating had been in academic circulation since 1805 — the Phase-1 anachronistic instinct was sound and the synthesis tag should reflect it.
